Claude.notes: Story openings are well-anchored to real events (Niépce 1826, ChatGPT launch, Attention Is All You Need, the lawyer ChatGPT brief). Key terms are defined cleanly. The protagonist arrives at insights mostly by being told rather than discovering — the narrative could push the learner to land on the insight more.
Claude.topIssue: Quizzes test recall (dates, parameter counts) more than judgment; capability delta is clear in concept but soft in lab deliverable.

Gemini.notes: Definitions of tokens, parameters, RLHF, Transformer, attention, and context window are accurate at the right depth. The 'parameters as mixing-board dials' analogy is informal but technically defensible. The 570 GB / 175B parameters / $100M training cost figures are widely reported approximations but presented as more precise than the underlying sources warrant.
Gemini.topIssue: Training-data and training-cost numbers (GPT-3 570 GB; GPT-4 >$100M) are estimates and should be flagged as such.

Perplexity.notes: Frontier-model examples stop at GPT-4 / GPT-4 Turbo and DALL·E 3 — no mention of Claude 4.x, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5/3, Llama 4, or the reasoning-model wave (o1/o3/R1) that reshaped the landscape after 2024. Context-window figure (128K) is now mid-tier rather than frontier (1M+ is common).
Perplexity.topIssue: Model/tooling table is two model generations stale; a 2026 learner will not see the systems they actually use.

Claude.notes: Stories — Word2Vec analogy, WGA strike, the Mata v. Avianca ChatGPT-cited-fake-cases brief, the rise of the prompt-engineer role — anchor each lesson well. Labs (vague vs. specific prompt, catching hallucinations, the three-pillars rewrite) translate the lesson into something the learner does.
Claude.topIssue: Quiz items lean on recall of definitions; one or two more 'which response is better, and why' items would tighten D5.c2.

Gemini.notes: Hallucination, bias, prompting techniques (few-shot, chain-of-thought-style framing) are defined correctly. Word2Vec is dated '2016' in the story scene; the original Mikolov paper is 2013 — the date appears to point to a later Google blog/Colab demo rather than the underlying technique.
Gemini.topIssue: Word2Vec date conflates the 2013 paper with a later popularization; readers will mis-cite this.

Perplexity.notes: The 'prompt engineer as a $300K job' framing was true in 2023 but has been largely absorbed into AI-engineer / agent-engineer / context-engineer roles by 2026. No mention of 2024–2026 writer cases (NYT v OpenAI, Authors Guild updates) or the shift toward longer-context and reasoning models that have reshaped how writers actually use AI today.
Perplexity.topIssue: 'Prompt engineer' framing and 2023 job-market snapshot are out of date — the role has consolidated and the tooling has moved on.

Claude.notes: Strongest narrative module in the course: Jason Allen's Colorado State Fair win, Andersen/McKernan/Ortiz class action, the Eliot Higgins Trump-arrest images, and the Refik Anadol studio adaptation each plant a clearly different question that the concept section then answers. Labs span prompt craft, ethical debate, detection, and planning — a real spread.
Claude.topIssue: Diffusion 'noise → image' explanation is good for the level but the protagonist is mostly an observer in three of four lessons.

Gemini.notes: Diffusion explanation is accurate at the lay level (forward noise / reverse denoise), though it elides the latent-space step that almost all modern systems use. Real-case citations (Allen, Andersen et al., Higgins/Trump) are factually correct and dated correctly.
Gemini.topIssue: Diffusion explanation glosses 'latent diffusion' — fine for Basic, but the term should at least appear in Key Terms.

Perplexity.notes: Image-model roster (DALL·E, Midjourney, Stable Diffusion) is correct but misses the 2024–2026 shift: GPT-image-1 / Sora-class, Flux, Ideogram, Recraft, and native-image generation inside chat assistants are absent. Detection-checklist heuristics still mostly work for hands/text artifacts but are weaker against current top-tier models.
Perplexity.topIssue: Tool roster and detection cues lag the 2024–2026 image-model generation; lab learners will be working with tools the lesson does not name.

Claude.notes: Story scenes (Sony CSL Daddy's Car, MuseNet, 2023 WGA/SAG strikes, Heart on My Sleeve) are well-chosen and emotionally weighted. Melody/harmony/rhythm framing gives the learner a clean spine. Labs are conversation-only — no in-page music generation or audio analysis, so the 'after this lesson, this learner can ___' verb is closer to 'discuss' than 'compose'.
Claude.topIssue: All four labs are discussions with the AI tutor; without a generation/listening step the capability delta is softer than other modules.

Gemini.notes: Technical claims (transformer-based audio generation, latent-audio approaches behind Suno/Udio, voice-cloning via reference audio) are correct at the level. 'Daddy's Car' attribution to Sony CSL Flow Machines (2016) is accurate. The melody/harmony/rhythm framing is musicologically defensible.
Gemini.topIssue: Suno/Udio architecture is described accurately but the underlying audio-token / diffusion split could be more explicit.

Perplexity.notes: Heart on My Sleeve and the WGA/SAG strikes are correct anchors but the music-AI landscape has moved fast: Suno v4/v5, Udio updates, ElevenLabs Music, Stable Audio 2, and the 2024 RIAA suits against Suno and Udio are absent. The Ghost Producer / voice-cloning discussion needs the post-2024 'no-AI clauses' that are now standard in major-label contracts.
Perplexity.topIssue: Music-AI roster and the RIAA v Suno/Udio litigation (June 2024) are missing — these are the defining 2024–2026 events.

Claude.notes: Strongest module in the course on D5: labs ask the learner to argue both sides, apply the four-factor fair-use test, audit their own creative output for copyrightability, and draft model legislation. The Rutkowski opening is one of the most concrete entries into the consent question in any AESOP course.
Claude.topIssue: Some lesson openings transition from story straight to legal exposition without the protagonist visibly working through the question.

Gemini.notes: Legal accuracy is excellent: Getty v Stability AI (UK and US tracks), Andersen et al. v Stability/Midjourney/DeviantArt, Authors Guild v OpenAI, USCO Zarya and Thaler v Perlmutter are all correctly characterized. EU AI Act transparency obligations and the NO FAKES / TRAIN Act bills are accurately summarized.
Gemini.topIssue: Thaler case discussion could note the DC Circuit affirmation rather than only the USCO decision, but the underlying summary is correct.

Perplexity.notes: Coverage extends to 2024 (EU AI Act, NO FAKES Act, TRAIN Act) which is good. Missing: NYT v OpenAI/Microsoft developments through 2025–2026, the EU AI Act's GPAI obligations taking effect in 2025, the USCO Part 2 / Part 3 reports on AI copyrightability, and the wave of opt-out tooling (Glaze/Nightshade adoption, Have I Been Trained, Spawning).
Perplexity.topIssue: Stops at 2024 — misses NYT v OpenAI developments, USCO multi-part reports, and the artist opt-out tooling ecosystem that has matured since.

Claude.notes: Strong on personal application: Hollie Mengert / Hollie+, LAION-5B, Zarya, and the WGA strike each illustrate a distinct slice of 'voice protection'. Labs (voice signature map, digital-presence audit, personal AI policy, defense plan) build cumulatively into a real artefact the learner leaves with.
Claude.topIssue: Lesson 3 narrative is thinner; the 'AI as tool vs. replacement' spectrum is asserted more than discovered through the story.

Gemini.notes: 'Style is not copyrightable' is correctly framed under current US doctrine. LAION-5B description (size, public-internet scraping, removal/re-release after CSAM findings) is accurate. The platform opt-out / consent-framework section is correct but uses a different layout convention (H2 sub-headings vs. the section-heading divs other modules use), which slightly hurts learner orientation.
Gemini.topIssue: Layout convention diverges from M1–M5; tab structure is intact but section visual hierarchy is less crisp.

Perplexity.notes: Hollie Mengert / LAION-5B / WGA / Zarya are correct anchors but all 2022–2023. The 2024–2026 reality — Cara migration, ArtStation's no-AI policy evolution, Glaze 2 / Nightshade adoption, Spawning's Do Not Train registry, platform-level opt-out flags becoming standard — is missing. 'Defense plan' artefacts the learner builds may already be partly obsolete.
Perplexity.topIssue: Personal-defense toolkit needs 2024–2026 tools (Glaze/Nightshade, Spawning, Cara, platform opt-out flags) — the lab outputs cite a pre-2024 ecosystem.

modules.module: M7: AI as a Creative Partner

Claude.notes: The Amplifier / Provocateur / Co-Author three-model framing is one of the cleanest curriculum payloads in the course — it gives the learner a vocabulary they can re-use anywhere. Sunspring, Allen, Heart on My Sleeve, and Zarya open each lesson with a concrete instance of one model going right or wrong.
Claude.topIssue: Four-element prompt framework (Subject/Style/Constraint/Perspective) is taught well but the lab evaluates it only via self-report.

Gemini.notes: Real cases (Sunspring/Goodwin/Benjamin 2016, Allen 2022, Ghostwriter977 2023, Kashtanova 2023) are factually accurate including the partial-rescission detail on Zarya. The three-model taxonomy is the lesson's own framework rather than a published one, but it is internally consistent.
Gemini.topIssue: Three-model taxonomy is original to AESOP; flagging it as a course framework rather than citing external literature would be more rigorous.

Perplexity.notes: Examples are all pre-2024. The prompt-engineering scaffolding is generic enough to age well, but the 'AI tools you can use' discussion in Lesson 3 needs the current generation: Claude Projects/Artefacts, GPT custom-GPTs and operators, Gemini Canvas, Suno v4/v5, Sora/Veo, Runway Gen-4 — none of which existed when this content was written.
Perplexity.topIssue: Lesson 3 tool roster predates the agentic / artefact / video-gen generation that defines 2024–2026 creative AI workflows.

modules.module: M8: The Future of Creative Work

Claude.notes: Final-lab '5-year creative plan' is the strongest capability delta in the course — the learner leaves with a personal artefact, not just an opinion. WGA, USCO, Heart on My Sleeve, and Adobe/College Board anchor jobs, ownership, skills, and education respectively.
Claude.topIssue: Some predictions in 'Three Futures' framing are presented without explicit uncertainty markers — a 'what could falsify this?' beat would tighten D2.c3.

Gemini.notes: Forward-looking claims are appropriately hedged. Adobe / College Board AP integration (2023–2024) is correctly described. WGA settlement terms on AI (writers retain credit, AI cannot be a 'writer') are accurate. The 'AI fluency' framework is sensible if not novel.
Gemini.topIssue: Mention of specific dollar figures or job-loss percentages should be sourced; current text reads as confident projection rather than cited estimate.

Perplexity.notes: Future-of-work module is the most exposed to currency drift: written-in-2024 projections about creative employment are now testable against 2025–2026 outcomes. Missing: the actual post-WGA-settlement landscape, the IATSE and SAG-AFTRA video-game-actor contracts, the 2025 layoff wave in concept art / illustration, and Adobe's Firefly indemnification rollout.
Perplexity.topIssue: Projections about creative careers are written as forecasts but should now be partly rewritten as outcomes — 2024-published predictions are checkable in 2026.
